stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \). What is the entry in row 1, column 1 of \( A^{-1} \)?
solution: For \( A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} \), \( A^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix} \). With \( ad-bc = ([[a]])([[d]])-([[b]])([[c]]) \), the \( (1,1) \) entry is \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{([[a]])([[d]])-([[b]])([[c]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This puts \( a \) in the \( (1,1) \) slot; the adjugate places \( d \) there (the diagonal entries are swapped).
This uses \( bc-ad \) in the denominator, flipping the sign of the determinant.
This uses entry \( b \) rather than \( d \) in the numerator.
This uses entry \( c \) rather than \( d \) in the numerator.
This has the wrong sign in the numerator.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \), so \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \) and \( A \) is invertible. What is \( \det(A^{-1}) \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the reciprocal and just gives \( \det(A) \) back.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This negates \( \det(A) \) instead of taking its reciprocal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \), so \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \) and \( A \) is invertible. What is \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{-1}) \)?
solution: Since \( A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det A}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix} \), its trace is \( \dfrac{a+d}{\det A} = \dfrac{[[a]]+[[d]]}{[[detA]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( \det(A) \), giving just \( \operatorname{tr}(A) \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies by \( \det(A) \) instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{pmatrix} \), so \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \) and \( A \) is invertible. Using the cofactor \( C_{11} = [[e]]\cdot[[i]] - [[f]]\cdot[[h]] \), what is the entry in row 1, column 1 of \( A^{-1} \)?
solution: The \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A^{-1} \) is the \( (1,1) \) cofactor divided by \( \det(A) \) (the \( (1,1) \) cofactor needs no transpose): \( \dfrac{C_{11}}{\det(A)} = \dfrac{[[e]]\cdot[[i]] - [[f]]\cdot[[h]]}{[[detA]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide the cofactor by \( \det(A) \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This uses the \( (1,2) \) cofactor's minor instead of the \( (1,1) \) cofactor's minor.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: To decide whether \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) has an inverse, compute \( \det(A) \) (a nonzero value means \( A^{-1} \) exists). What is \( \det(A) \)?
distractor_rationales: This adds the diagonal products instead of subtracting them.
This is the negative of the determinant (the two products were swapped).
This sums all four entries instead of computing \( ad-bc \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
